siteFooter.tagline: Solana 上のシード型ボクセルワールドプロトコル。
siteFooter.navigationAria: フッターナビゲーション
siteFooter.socialAria: NiceChunk ソーシャルリンク

meta.title: NiceChunk Civilization Codex
meta.description: NiceChunk Civilization Codex: players write, read, sign, validate, and execute world rules without project-side privilege.

hero.title: Civilization Codex
hero.subtitle: Players do not only play the world. They write the laws of the world.
hero.body: NiceChunk civilization rules are designed for a public on-chain world where resources, equipment, privileges, and world parameters cannot appear from nowhere.

actions.comingSoon: On-chain interaction is planned for a later release.

entry.title: Start with the promise, then inspect the proof.
entry.body: The civilization page is a deep technical document. Use one of the reader tracks, then follow every claim down to the matching hash, PDA, validator, receipt, or rejection reason.

player.label: Player path
player.title: What will this rule change for me?
player.body: Start from plain language, personal impact, signing meaning, and the choice between agree, reject, abstain, or challenge.

builder.label: Builder path
builder.title: How does a rule become executable state?
builder.body: Follow the machine patch, target module, validator gates, config PDA write, and execution receipt.

auditor.label: Auditor path
auditor.title: Can I prove it without trusting NiceChunk?
auditor.body: Jump to decoded PDAs, hash recomputation, signature recounts, challenge evidence, and event receipts.

promise.label: Human promise
promise.title: A rule starts as readable law.
promise.body: Players should understand the change before seeing code or signing power.

machine.label: Machine proof
machine.title: Text and patch stay hash-bound.
machine.body: Any edited word or byte changes the identity that signatures and validators check.

pda.label: Public PDA
pda.title: The same identity must be decodable.
pda.body: Rule books, signatures, snapshots, challenges, and registries should be public accounts.

failure.label: Failure mode
failure.title: Bad rules should fail loudly.
failure.body: A rejection reason or challenge receipt is part of trust, not an error to hide.

trustLadder.title: A civilization rule should climb six public proofs before it becomes law.
trustLadder.body: This is the shortest way to judge NiceChunk civilization: a player reads a promise, a reviewer checks the proof object, and the contract rejects the rule if any rung is missing.

promise.title: Readable promise
promise.body: The rule must first say what changes for players in plain language.
promise.failure: If players cannot understand it, the interface should keep it in draft.

identity.title: Frozen identity
identity.body: The readable text and machine patch must share stable hashes before signatures count.
identity.failure: If either hash changes, old signatures no longer apply.

power.title: Receipt-backed power
power.body: Civilization power should come from public participation receipts, then be frozen for the rule epoch.
power.failure: If power cannot be recounted, the threshold result is not credible.

validator.title: Deterministic validation
validator.body: The validator dry-run proves scope, conservation, old asset policy, and writable PDA families before execution.
validator.failure: If validation fails, the rule emits a named rejection instead of mutating state.

execute.title: Permissionless execution
execute.body: After threshold, cooling, and challenge checks pass, any caller should be able to submit execute_rule.
execute.failure: If an admin signer is required, the civilization promise is not complete.

receipt.title: Public receipt
receipt.body: Success and failure both end in decoded accounts or events so another reader can replay the result.
receipt.failure: If no receipt exists, the website claim is only a claim.

toc.title: Choose a reading path before entering the full civilization codex.
toc.body: The page is intentionally deep. Use this directory to jump between beginner explanations, rule safety, signing impact, civic power, execution proofs, PDA transparency, and future civilization design.

trustLadder.title: Trust Ladder
trustLadder.body: See the six proofs a rule must pass before becoming law.

termDecoder.title: Term Decoder
termDecoder.body: Translate jargon into plain meaning and proof objects.

plainModel.title: Plain Model
plainModel.body: Understand the civilization layer as a public rule engine.

readerPath.title: Reader Path
readerPath.body: See what a player should check before signing.

ruleAuthoring.title: Rule Authoring
ruleAuthoring.body: Turn a player idea into a safe, template-bound, dry-run rule book.

trustJourney.title: Trust Journey
trustJourney.body: Follow play, proof, power, proposal, and audit in one line.

implementationBoundary.title: Implementation Boundary
implementationBoundary.body: Separate what is live, what is designed next, and what is not claimed.

lawLayers.title: Two Law Layers
lawLayers.body: Compare Natural Law protection with Civilization Law evolution.

ruleClassifier.title: Rule Classifier
ruleClassifier.body: Learn which changes use 50%, 90%, or direct rejection.

conservationLedger.title: Conservation Ledger
conservationLedger.body: Trace resource origin, input burn, recipe version, output receipt, and audit balance.

naturalLaw.title: Natural Law Warning
naturalLaw.body: See why world-level changes need stronger prompts, validators, and consensus.

civilizationPower.title: Civilization Power
civilizationPower.body: Understand why power comes from provable participation instead of wallet count.

powerSnapshot.title: Power Snapshot
powerSnapshot.body: See how receipts become capped, frozen, recountable rule power.

threatModel.title: Threat Model
threatModel.body: Review how hostile rules fail before state writes.

challengeDesk.title: Challenge Desk
challengeDesk.body: Follow how suspicious rules become evidence, pauses, resolutions, and lessons.

caseWalkthrough.title: Bronze Age Case
caseWalkthrough.body: Trace one rule from player story to executable patch.

impactSimulator.title: Impact Simulator
impactSimulator.body: Preview personal, world, economic, and technical impact.

signingSafety.title: Before You Sign
signingSafety.body: Confirm hashes, risk, frozen power, and the exact side your wallet signs.

decisionSplit.title: Decision Split
decisionSplit.body: Separate agree, reject, abstain, and challenge so players know which action pauses execution.

civicPower.title: Civic Power Ledger
civicPower.body: See how provable play becomes non-transferable power.

ruleLifecycle.title: Rule Lifecycle
ruleLifecycle.body: Follow a rule book from draft, reading, cooling, challenge, and execution.

ruleStructure.title: Rule Structure
ruleStructure.body: Inspect the fields that bind readable intent to executable patch data.

technicalFlow.title: Technical Flow
technicalFlow.body: See why execution applies a verified patch instead of vague instructions.

ruleValidator.title: RuleValidator
ruleValidator.body: Review deterministic gates before a rule can write config PDAs.

executionProof.title: Execution Proof
executionProof.body: Inspect signatures, accounts, failure paths, and writes.

executionReceipts.title: Execution Receipts
executionReceipts.body: Replay success, rejection, indexer, and later accountability receipts.

pdaTransparency.title: PDA Transparency
pdaTransparency.body: Browse the account families behind public truth.

independentVerification.title: Independent Verification
independentVerification.body: Verify claims from PDA owner, hashes, recounts, simulations, and receipts.

versionedEvolution.title: Versioned Evolution
versionedEvolution.body: Understand how rules advance without erasing previous versions or assets.

citizenRoles.title: Citizen Roles
citizenRoles.body: Map explorer, miner, builder, guardian, and auditor duties to public proof.

civilizationDesign.title: Civilization Design
civilizationDesign.body: Read the future society engine and PDA blueprint.

civicWorks.title: Civic Works
civicWorks.body: Connect settlement projects, public materials, guardians, and rule effects.

buildSpec.title: Build Spec
buildSpec.body: Use the page as an implementation brief for coding.

auditTrail.title: Audit Trail
auditTrail.body: Trace each future instruction through hashes, changed PDAs, and events.

pdaBinding.title: PDA Binding
pdaBinding.body: Connect the civilization design to real NiceChunk account surfaces.

targetModules.title: Target Modules
targetModules.body: Review which bounded config modules a rule is allowed to touch.

ruleExamples.title: Rule Examples
ruleExamples.body: Compare Natural Law protection rules with Civilization Law advancement rules.

executionModel.title: Execution Model
executionModel.body: See why automatic execution means permissionless contract checks.

ruleBooks.title: Mock Rule Books
ruleBooks.body: Inspect local mock rules shaped like future on-chain accounts.

futureContractDirection.title: Future Contract Direction
futureContractDirection.body: Read how the civilization program should mutate validated config PDAs.

glossary.title: The same words should be readable as player promises and technical evidence.
glossary.body: Civilization becomes easier to trust when every important word has two meanings on the page: a plain explanation for players and a concrete proof object for reviewers.
glossary.stackTitle: How to read technical words without guessing

checks.plainFirst: Start with the player promise: what does this word mean in gameplay?
checks.proofSecond: Then show the proof object: account, hash, snapshot, validator, or event.
checks.sameIdentity: Keep the plain word and the proof object bound to the same rule identity.
checks.replayable: Make the term replayable so another reader can recompute the same answer.
checks.shareable: Expose enough detail for a link, screenshot, or decoded account panel to prove the claim.

ruleBook.label: Rule Book
ruleBook.title: A public proposal, not a chat message.
ruleBook.body: For players, it is the readable law they review. For auditors, it is the account that binds text, patch, author, risk, threshold, and lifecycle state.

pda.label: PDA
pda.title: A public account with a predictable address.
pda.body: The page should treat each PDA as a verifiable shelf where rules, signatures, configs, receipts, and challenges can be decoded.

hash.label: Hash
hash.title: A fingerprint for text or machine patch bytes.
hash.body: If a readable rule or patch changes, its hash changes. That lets signers verify they are reviewing the exact same object.

snapshot.label: Snapshot
snapshot.title: A frozen view of citizen power at one epoch.
snapshot.body: Snapshots stop last-minute account movement from changing the weight behind an already published rule.

validator.label: Validator
validator.title: The deterministic safety gate before writes.
validator.body: The validator parses the patch, checks scope and conservation, and rejects unsafe changes before any config PDA is written.

event.label: Event
event.title: The public receipt after execution or rejection.
event.body: Events let indexers and players see what happened, why it happened, and which rule identity produced the result.

concept.title: Civilization is the rule engine above the playable world.
concept.body: A new player can read it as a public rule book. A technical reviewer can read it as a bounded state machine: hashed proposals, snapshot signatures, validator gates, and config PDA writes.

publicRulebook.title: Rules are readable before they are signed.
publicRulebook.body: A civilization rule must explain what it changes, why it matters, which old assets are protected, and what happens after execution.

boundedMachine.title: The executable part is constrained.
boundedMachine.body: The machine patch targets known configuration modules. It is not a free-form command that can rewrite contracts or secretly mint resources.

noSilentAdmin.title: No silent administrator path.
noSilentAdmin.body: Thresholds, cooling windows, signatures, validators, and PDA writes are designed to be inspectable instead of hidden behind project-side discretion.

readerGuide.title: A player should know how to judge a rule before signing it.
readerGuide.body: The civilization page must work as a rule-reading interface, not only a technical manifesto. Every proposal should answer simple player questions and expose the exact evidence experts need to verify the same answer.

player.title: Can I explain the change in one sentence?
player.body: A nontechnical player should understand the rule without reading Rust, PDA bytes, or governance jargon.

checks.plainChange: What changes in the world after this rule?
checks.personalImpact: Can it affect my backpack, items, resources, or future rewards?
checks.signChoice: Am I signing agree, reject, or abstain with a clear reason?

builder.title: Can I map the rule to a bounded module?
builder.body: A developer should see exactly which gameplay surface will change and which surfaces are intentionally out of scope.

checks.patchDiff: What field path, old_value_hash, and new_value_hash are being proposed?
checks.executionPath: Which instruction can execute it and which account family can be written?

auditor.title: Can I independently reject a bad rule?
auditor.body: A reviewer should be able to reproduce the safety result from public hashes, PDA reads, signatures, and event logs.

checks.hashes: Do text_hash and patch_hash identify the same rule across every step?
checks.pdaWrites: Are all writable PDAs declared before execution?
checks.rejectionProof: Is there a named failure path if a threshold, hash, scope, or asset rule fails?

whatChanges.title: What changes?
whatChanges.body: The rule must describe the gameplay result in plain language before showing machine data.

affectedSurface.title: Which surface is affected?
affectedSurface.body: The target module should be explicit: smelting, forging, resource rules, block parameters, market policy, or another declared surface.

oldAssets.title: Can old assets be harmed?
oldAssets.body: If existing player-owned assets can be seized, downgraded, or rewritten, the proposal must move to the stronger Natural Law path.

hashProof.title: What proves identity?
hashProof.body: The readable rule and executable patch must be bound by stable hashes so signers can verify they are signing the same object.

thresholdPath.title: Which threshold applies?
thresholdPath.body: Civilization Law and Natural Law use different consensus levels, cooling periods, and challenge expectations.

failurePath.title: How can it fail safely?
failurePath.body: A credible rule explains how execution is rejected when hashes are stale, scope is wrong, signatures are insufficient, or assets are unsafe.

authoring.title: A good civilization system helps ordinary players write safe rules without hiding the machine truth.
authoring.body: The authoring flow should start from a plain player idea, force it through known templates, preview impact, generate a hash-bound machine patch, dry-run the validator, and only then freeze a rule book for signatures.
authoring.stackTitle: Authoring gates before a rule can ask for power

checks.plainIntent: The proposal starts as a one-sentence player promise that can be understood without code.
checks.templateFit: The UI must choose a known rule template so the contract can map the idea to a bounded module.
checks.boundedPatch: The generated patch declares module, operation, field path, old hash, new hash, and migration mode.
checks.dryRun: Before publication, the validator dry-run shows accept or reject with the exact failed gate.
checks.freezeBeforeSign: Once published for signatures, text and patch bytes are frozen so signers review one stable object.

production.title: Production unlock
production.body: For rules that add a recipe, processing route, or future-only material path without changing old assets.

settlement.title: Settlement project
settlement.body: For public works where players contribute material receipts and receive a bounded settlement effect.

safety.title: World safety amendment
safety.body: For constitutional rules that protect conservation, ownership, no-admin privilege, and old asset safety.

knowledge.title: Knowledge record
knowledge.body: For discoveries, era records, public explanations, and world facts that should become decoded civic memory.

intent.title: Readable promise
intent.body: The exact text players read is hashed and shown before signatures count.

template.title: Template identity
template.body: The selected template prevents an author from smuggling arbitrary behavior into a vague proposal.

module.title: Writable surface
module.body: The target modules tell reviewers which config families can be affected and which are out of scope.

patch.title: Machine patch
patch.body: The executable bytes are hashed separately from the readable text and compared before signing.

dryRun.title: Safety result
dryRun.body: The dry-run hash binds the validator result, touched accounts, and rejection reason if the draft fails.

freeze.title: Frozen rule book
freeze.body: The final rule PDA should become immutable for the voting window so signatures cannot be redirected.

journey.title: A newcomer should see one continuous path from playing the world to trusting a rule.
journey.body: The civilization page should not start from abstract governance. It should show how ordinary actions become public evidence, how evidence becomes civilization power, and how that power can approve only bounded, inspectable changes.
journey.stackTitle: How simple play becomes trustworthy governance

translations.plainAction: Start with the player's plain action: explore, mine, craft, build, trade, protect, or write.
translations.publicEvidence: Convert that action into public evidence: origin hashes, account state, events, and decoded PDA fields.
translations.powerSnapshot: Only mature, filtered participation becomes snapshot power for rule signatures.
translations.boundedChange: A signature never gives unlimited authority; it approves one bounded rule object and one declared patch.
translations.replayableResult: After execution, the result must be replayable from rule text, patch hash, accounts, and event logs.

enter.title: Enter the world
enter.body: A beginner starts with a wallet, a seed chunk, and visible resources. The page should explain that this is already public world state, not a private game save.

gather.title: Gather material proof
gather.body: Mining or finding resources creates provenance: which chunk, which generation rule, which resource, and which account recorded it.

craft.title: Craft through rules
craft.body: Smelting, forging, and backpack changes should point to recipe versions so players know why an item exists.

settle.title: Join a settlement
settle.body: Players can cooperate around chunks, production, guardians, and public projects, turning individual activity into civic state.

propose.title: Propose or sign a rule
propose.body: A rule uses the player's readable reason, frozen power, target module, hashes, and threshold path before it can execute.

verify.title: Verify the result
verify.body: The final state should be visible through decoded PDAs and events so a beginner can trust the outcome and an auditor can replay it.

boundary.title: Credibility starts with saying what is live, what is designed, and what is not claimed.
boundary.body: This page is a technical product direction for the civilization system. It separates the current public UI model from the future on-chain governance contract so reviewers can judge feasibility without guessing.

items.one: Readable law layers, lifecycle, examples, and technical account model are published on the website.
items.two: Mock rule books show the future data shape without pretending they are already final chain governance.
items.three: The page connects civilization design to existing NiceChunk modules such as smelting, forging, resource rules, and config PDAs.

items.one: rule_book_pda, rule_signature_pda, citizen_state_pda, and rule_registry_pda can make the model directly auditable.
items.two: RuleValidator should constrain machine patches before any config PDA write.
items.three: execute_rule should be permissionless after threshold, cooling, and challenge conditions pass.

items.one: This page does not claim mainnet governance is complete today.
items.two: It does not claim arbitrary program upgrades can be safely controlled by text votes.
items.three: It does not claim old player assets can be retroactively changed by ordinary Civilization Law.

entry.title: Civilization loop in one view
entry.caption: A player action becomes public proof, public proof becomes civic power, and only validated rules can write future config PDAs.

trustLadder.title: Six-rung trust ladder
trustLadder.caption: Every rule must move from readable intent to frozen identity, accountable power, deterministic validation, narrow execution, and public receipt.

system.title: From player action to world law
system.caption: Every important step leaves a public object or public hash.

glossary.title: Plain word to proof object map
glossary.caption: A reader can move from simple language to the exact account, hash, snapshot, validator, or event that proves it.

readerGuide.title: From plain question to verifiable proof
readerGuide.caption: The same rule should be understandable as a player story and auditable as deterministic state.

badges.sameRule: one rule identity across text, patch, and event

authoring.title: Idea-to-signable-rule pipeline
authoring.caption: Players write in plain language, but every published rule must end as a frozen, hash-bound, validator-tested object.

journey.title: New player trust journey
journey.caption: A simple player story and the technical proof chain should describe the same world state.

classifier.title: Rule classification decision tree
classifier.caption: Classification happens before voting so popularity cannot bypass asset safety or world invariants.

conservation.title: Resource conservation proof loop
conservation.caption: A rule can change production only when every output can be traced back to origin, input burn, recipe version, and audit balance.

power.title: Civilization power is a filtered participation snapshot
power.caption: Participation adds signal; anti-abuse filters reduce noise before voting power is frozen.

snapshot.title: Receipt-to-snapshot integrity pipeline
snapshot.caption: Participation becomes voting weight only after receipt checks, normalization, caps, epoch freeze, and public recountability.

threatModel.title: Threat-to-defense map
threatModel.caption: Every dangerous path should meet a visible defense before it can become state.

badges.untrusted: assume every rule can be hostile

challenge.title: Challenge-to-resolution path
challenge.caption: A bad rule should move from suspicion to evidence, paused execution, public resolution, and a replayable lesson.

caseWalkthrough.title: Bronze Age rule execution map
caseWalkthrough.caption: The same proposal moves from player story to machine patch to audited on-chain state.

impact.title: Rule impact simulation path
impact.caption: The same dry run should explain player impact, PDA reads, validator gates, and the final sign-or-reject decision.

signing.title: Signature safety chain
signing.caption: A signature becomes trustworthy when the wallet action, risk acknowledgement, PDA write, and public recount all bind to the same rule identity.

decision.title: Four-way decision routing
decision.caption: A player chooses a side for threshold math, but only a challenge with evidence pauses execution.

reputation.title: Action-to-civic-power pipeline
reputation.caption: Civilization power is assembled from receipts, normalized into a snapshot, then used only for bounded rule signatures.

execution.title: Hash-bound execution path
execution.caption: Readers can compare text_hash and patch_hash before signing.

validator.title: RuleValidator gate sequence
validator.caption: Unsafe proposals fail before signatures can become executable state changes.

transaction.title: Permissionless execution transaction
transaction.caption: Any caller can execute, but only if the transaction proves the same rule, same snapshot, same hashes, and same expected account state.

receipts.title: Execution receipt accountability chain
receipts.caption: Success and failure both produce public evidence, so the website, indexer, and skeptical reader can replay the same outcome.

transparency.title: Public evidence browser map
transparency.caption: Every claim on the civilization page should point to a decoded account, event, or rejection reason.

badges.noTrust: do not trust the website alone

verification.title: Claim-to-proof verification path
verification.caption: A public claim becomes trustworthy only when another reader can independently recompute the same proof chain.

badges.noWebsiteTrust: do not trust the website alone

versioning.title: Rule version chain
versioning.caption: A module can advance to a new config without making previous assets or audit trails disappear.

roles.title: Citizen role-to-proof matrix
roles.caption: A role is credible only when it maps to public work, public receipts, public duties, and replayable power inputs.

badges.identity: one citizen can hold many duties

design.title: Civilization gameplay-to-PDA map
design.caption: The page defines the future coding model: player actions create public state, public state unlocks eras, and rules change only bounded config.

badges.pda: decoded PDA anchor for every claim

civic.title: Settlement public works loop
civic.caption: Materials, citizens, guardians, and rules form one inspectable loop before a settlement receives a benefit.

auditTrail.title: Instruction-to-event audit trail
auditTrail.caption: A reviewer should be able to replay the same rule from published hash to final config write.

binding.title: Civilization-to-PDA binding map
binding.caption: Future rules should write narrow config PDAs that existing programs can read or that migration validators can safely apply.

implementationTrace.title: Every instruction should leave a replayable audit trail.
implementationTrace.body: The future civilization program should be easy to reconstruct from public data: which instruction was called, which PDA changed, which hash was checked, which event was emitted, and why a rule was accepted or rejected.

invariants.hashIdentity: text_hash and patch_hash must identify the same rule through publish, sign, finalize, execute, and event logs.
invariants.snapshotReplay: A threshold recount must be possible from signature PDAs and snapshot_epoch without trusting a website cache.
invariants.singleSignature: One voter can have one effective signature per rule unless a later replacement path is explicitly designed.
invariants.noPrivilegedExecute: execute_rule must not require a project wallet, admin signer, or hidden server authority.
invariants.boundedWrite: A successful execution writes only the declared registry/config PDA family and never arbitrary account data.
invariants.rejectionReason: Every rejected execution path should have a stable, documented reason that users and indexers can display.

publishRule.title: publish_rule
publishRule.body: Creates a rule book account and freezes the readable text hash, machine patch hash, law type, risk level, target module list, and snapshot epoch.
publishRule.input: rule_id, text_hash, patch_hash, law_type, risk_level, target_modules, snapshot_epoch
publishRule.output: RuleBook PDA initialized; RulePublished event emitted.

signRule.title: sign_rule
signRule.body: Creates or updates the voter's signature PDA for one rule, using civilization power from the declared snapshot epoch.
signRule.input: rule_id, voter, side, snapshot_epoch, expected_text_hash, expected_patch_hash
signRule.output: RuleSignature PDA written; RuleSigned event emitted.

finalizeThreshold.title: finalize_threshold
finalizeThreshold.body: Recounts public signatures, stores final yes/no/abstain power, and marks whether the threshold is met.
finalizeThreshold.output: Rule tally finalized; ThresholdFinalized event emitted.

challengeRule.title: challenge_rule
challengeRule.body: Records a challenge during the challenge window with a public evidence hash and blocks execution until resolved.
challengeRule.output: Challenge PDA written; RuleChallenged event emitted.

executeRule.title: execute_rule
executeRule.body: Runs the validator, checks current target config hash, then writes the accepted registry/config PDA or emits a named rejection reason.
executeRule.output: RuleExecuted or RuleRejected event emitted; config write only if all gates pass.

rulePublished.title: RulePublished
rulePublished.body: Signals that a rule is now readable and hash-bound.
rulePublished.payload: rule_id, author, text_hash, patch_hash, law_type, risk_level, snapshot_epoch

ruleSigned.title: RuleSigned
ruleSigned.body: Signals that a citizen signature changed threshold math.
ruleSigned.payload: rule_id, voter, side, power_at_snapshot, snapshot_epoch, signature_pda

thresholdFinalized.title: ThresholdFinalized
thresholdFinalized.body: Signals that signatures were recounted and the rule can move to cooling or blocked state.
thresholdFinalized.payload: rule_id, yes_power, no_power, abstain_power, threshold_met, finalized_slot

ruleChallenged.title: RuleChallenged
ruleChallenged.body: Signals that execution is paused for review with public evidence.

ruleExecuted.title: RuleExecuted
ruleExecuted.body: Signals that a validated patch wrote a narrow config or registry result.
ruleExecuted.payload: rule_id, executor, target_module, old_value_hash, new_value_hash, written_pda

ruleRejected.title: RuleRejected
ruleRejected.body: Signals that execution failed without writing config state.

executionProof.title: A rule becomes credible only when its signatures, accounts, and failure paths are inspectable.
executionProof.body: This is the bridge between a readable proposal and a trustworthy state change: frozen power snapshots, signature PDAs, deterministic account reads, validator rejection reasons, and a narrow registry write.

snapshotFrozen.title: Signer power is frozen before signatures count.
snapshotFrozen.body: The signature stores snapshot_epoch and power_at_snapshot. Later mining, trading, or wallet movement cannot rewrite the voting weight of an already published rule.

signatureAccount.title: Every vote is a public account, not a hidden database row.
signatureAccount.body: A rule_signature_pda should bind voter, rule_id, side, power, timestamp, text_hash, and patch_hash so observers can recount the threshold.

executeAccounts.title: execute_rule reads proofs before it writes results.
executeAccounts.body: The transaction reads the rule book, signature totals, citizen snapshot metadata, current target config hash, and validator scope before writing a registry or module config PDA.

publicRejection.title: Rejected rules should fail with named reasons.
publicRejection.body: A failed execution should expose why it failed: stale hash, missing threshold, expired window, wrong module, conservation break, or asset safety violation.

ruleBook.title: Rule book account
ruleBook.body: Stores readable metadata, law type, risk level, target modules, text_hash, patch_hash, snapshot_epoch, and lifecycle timestamps.

signature.title: Signature account
signature.body: Stores a voter's side and frozen civilization power for one rule so votes can be independently recounted.

citizen.title: Citizen state account
citizen.body: Stores participation-derived power inputs and epoch snapshots used by rule signatures.

registry.title: Rule registry account
registry.body: Stores accepted rule versions, supersession links, execution slots, and active module policy references.

moduleConfig.title: Module config account
moduleConfig.body: Stores the narrow result that gameplay programs read, such as recipe tables, drop tables, fee policy, or block parameters.

staleHash.title: Stale old_value_hash
staleHash.body: The target config changed after the proposal was published, so the rule must be reviewed again instead of overwriting newer state.

thresholdMissing.title: Threshold not reached
thresholdMissing.body: The yes power does not satisfy the required Civilization Law or Natural Law threshold at the frozen snapshot.

snapshotChanged.title: Invalid snapshot window
snapshotChanged.body: The rule is using an expired, missing, or mismatched snapshot epoch, so signature power cannot be trusted.

assetViolation.title: Old asset safety violation
assetViolation.body: The patch would seize, downgrade, or rewrite existing player-owned assets without the stronger Natural Law path.

conservationFailure.title: Resource conservation failure
conservationFailure.body: The patch creates outputs without bounded inputs, generated sources, or an allowed transformation rule.

wrongScope.title: Wrong module scope
wrongScope.body: The patch tries to mutate a module or field path outside the declared target_module list.

receipts.title: After execution, the rule should leave a receipt that players can replay forever.
receipts.body: A trustworthy civilization system does not stop at a green success message. It should publish before-and-after hashes, written PDA addresses, event payloads, rejection reasons, indexer confirmations, and later supersession or challenge references.

checks.sameIdentity: The receipt must bind the same rule_id, text_hash, patch_hash, snapshot_epoch, and target module that players signed.
checks.beforeAfterHash: A successful write must expose old_value_hash and new_value_hash so the exact state transition can be replayed.
checks.namedFailure: A rejected execution should emit a named rejection code instead of disappearing as a generic failed transaction.
checks.indexerParity: The website view and independent indexers should derive the same status from the same event and PDA set.
checks.futureDispute: Later challenges, supersession rules, or compatibility notes should link back to the original receipt.

transaction.title: Transaction is submitted
transaction.body: Any caller can submit execute_rule, but the transaction must carry the same rule identity and expected state.

precheck.title: Hashes are checked first
precheck.body: The contract verifies text_hash, patch_hash, old_value_hash, snapshot_epoch, challenge state, and target module before any write.

write.title: Only narrow PDA state changes
write.body: If every gate passes, the transaction writes a registry or module config PDA and records the new active hash.

event.title: One public event explains the result
event.body: Success and rejection both produce an event payload so users can see what happened and why.

index.title: Indexers rebuild the same status
index.body: The UI should not invent state. It should show the same result an independent indexer can rebuild from accounts and events.

accountability.title: Future rules can point backward
accountability.body: A later challenge, compatibility note, or superseding version should cite the original receipt instead of rewriting history.

transparency.title: Players should be able to inspect civilization truth without trusting the website.
transparency.body: The future PDA browser should decode the same public accounts that the contract reads: rules, signatures, citizen snapshots, module registries, config PDAs, challenges, and execution events.

checks.decode: Decode raw PDA bytes into named fields instead of showing opaque base64.
checks.compare: Compare text_hash, patch_hash, old_value_hash, and active_config_hash side by side.
checks.recount: Recount threshold math from signature accounts and snapshot_epoch.
checks.simulate: Run read-only validator simulation before users sign or execute.
checks.watch: Show RuleExecuted, RuleRejected, and RuleChallenged events as a public timeline.

ruleLedger.label: Rule ledger
ruleLedger.title: Every proposal has a public identity.
ruleLedger.body: The browser should show author, law type, risk level, target modules, readable hash, patch hash, cooldown, and current lifecycle state.

signatureLedger.label: Signature ledger
signatureLedger.title: Every vote can be recounted.
signatureLedger.body: A user should see which accounts signed, which side they chose, and how much frozen civilization power each signature contributed.

citizenSnapshot.label: Citizen snapshot
citizenSnapshot.title: Civilization power is inspectable input.
citizenSnapshot.body: The snapshot panel should explain mature identity, activity, caps, decay, and the final power used by a rule.

configRegistry.label: Config registry
configRegistry.title: Accepted rules point to active module state.
configRegistry.body: The registry should expose which rule version controls smelting, forging, resource drops, block parameters, or other module configs.

assetSafety.label: Asset safety
assetSafety.title: Player-owned assets stay visible and protected.
assetSafety.body: When a rule claims it does not touch old assets, the browser should show relevant read-only backpack or market account checks.

challengeCourt.label: Challenge court
challengeCourt.title: Objections become public evidence, not private arguments.
challengeCourt.body: Challenges should have account state, evidence hashes, expiry, resolution status, and the failed gate if execution is rejected.

verification.title: A third party should be able to prove or reject every civilization claim without asking NiceChunk.
verification.body: The page should teach a skeptical reader how to move from a website claim to decoded PDA ownership, recomputed hashes, signature recounts, validator dry-runs, and final event receipts.
verification.stackTitle: Rules for a proof that does not depend on trust

checks.independentSource: Start from a public RPC response, explorer, or exported account dump, not from a private website database.
checks.sameOwner: Confirm each PDA is owned by the expected program and derived from the documented seed family.
checks.hashRecompute: Recompute text_hash, patch_hash, and config hashes from bytes before accepting a readable claim.
checks.thresholdReplay: Recount signatures from rule_signature_pda accounts at the frozen snapshot_epoch.
checks.publicReceipt: End with a public event or rejection reason so the result can be shared and replayed.

claim.title: Name the exact claim.
claim.body: A reader starts with one sentence from the page, such as which rule is active or why a proposal was rejected.

account.title: Open the decoded account.
account.body: The verifier checks owner program, PDA seeds, account discriminator, rule_id, module, and lifecycle fields.

hash.title: Recompute the hashes.
hash.body: Readable text, machine patch bytes, and config state must produce the same hashes shown by the interface.

recount.title: Recount power from signatures.
recount.body: Threshold math should be reconstructed from signature accounts and citizen snapshots, not accepted as a displayed number.

simulate.title: Run the validator dry-run.
simulate.body: Before a claim is trusted, the same patch should pass scope, conservation, asset safety, and expected-state checks.

receipt.title: Check the public outcome.
receipt.body: The final proof is an event, written PDA, or named rejection reason that another reviewer can replay.

versioning.title: Civilization advances by creating a public version chain, not by erasing old rules.
versioning.body: A credible world needs historical continuity. When a new rule replaces an old one, the registry should preserve old hashes, effective epochs, supersession links, and asset compatibility notes.

principles.appendOnly: Rule history is append-only: a new active rule links to the previous version instead of deleting it.
principles.effectiveEpoch: Each version has an effective_epoch so players know which rule applied when an action happened.
principles.compatibility: Compatibility policy explains how old backpacks, recipes, listings, and crafted items remain valid.
principles.supersession: Supersession links show why v2 replaced v1 and which rule approved the change.
principles.auditReplay: A reviewer can replay v1 behavior, v2 behavior, and the transition between them from public hashes.

activeV1.title: Active version
activeV1.body: The current module config remains readable with rule_id, config hash, effective epoch, and owner program.

proposalV2.title: Proposed replacement
proposalV2.body: A new rule proposes a patch that references the current active hash and declares the superseded version.

compatibility.title: Compatibility policy
compatibility.body: The validator records whether old assets keep v1 semantics, migrate forward, or require Natural Law.

activeV2.title: New active version
activeV2.body: After execution, new gameplay reads v2 while old history, events, and compatibility notes remain inspectable.

roles.title: Civilization roles should describe public duties, not private authority.
roles.body: A player can become an explorer, miner, forger, builder, guardian, or auditor through public receipts. Roles explain how a citizen helps the world and which proof object backs that contribution; they should never become hidden admin permissions.

checks.notPermission: A role is not an admin permission. It cannot bypass ownership, conservation, validator gates, or rule thresholds.
checks.receiptBacked: Role contribution must be backed by public receipts, not by website badges or private reputation rows.
checks.rotatable: Citizens can grow into multiple roles over time; the system should record history instead of forcing a permanent class.
checks.abuseBounded: Each role needs caps, maturity checks, and anomaly filters before it can affect civilization power.
checks.publicBenefit: A role should benefit the world through maps, resources, recipes, public works, coverage, or audits that other players can inspect.

explorer.label: Explorer
explorer.title: Finds and explains the frontier.
explorer.body: Explorers turn chunk discovery, route mapping, anomaly reports, and world knowledge into public signals for later rules.

miner.label: Miner
miner.title: Proves where resources came from.
miner.body: Miners connect generated sources, extraction receipts, and conservation checks so materials are trusted before they enter production.

forger.label: Forger
forger.title: Turns resources into durable artifacts.
forger.body: Forgers publish input consumption, recipe version, output receipt, and equipment lineage instead of only showing a balance.

builder.label: Builder
builder.title: Contributes to shared public works.
builder.body: Builders turn materials and labor into milestone receipts for settlements, roads, furnaces, archives, markets, and civic machines.

guardian.label: Guardian
guardian.title: Provides service coverage that can be verified.
guardian.body: Guardians submit coverage proofs, region commitments, uptime records, and penalty evidence for territory or service benefits.

auditor.label: Auditor
auditor.title: Protects the civilization from unsafe rules.
auditor.body: Auditors compare hashes, run dry-runs, file challenges, and preserve public evidence when proposals are misleading or unsafe.

design.title: The civilization system should become a playable society engine, not only a voting page.
design.body: Future coding should treat civilization as the public layer that connects eras, citizens, settlements, production, knowledge, and rule execution. Each design object needs a readable player promise and a decoded PDA anchor.

principles.readablePromise: Every feature starts with a player-readable promise before it becomes account data.
principles.pdaAnchor: Every promise must map to a PDA, event, hash, or deterministic rejection reason.
principles.materialContinuity: Civilization progress must respect material provenance: resources, recipes, and crafted items cannot appear from nowhere.
principles.forwardUnlocks: New eras and technologies should unlock future actions without silently rewriting old player history.
principles.publicDecoder: The website should eventually decode each civilization account beside the rule that produced it.

era.label: Era
era.title: Eras are public unlock layers.
era.body: Stone, bronze, iron, energy, automation, or future eras should be activated by proven world activity and accepted rule versions, not by a private switch.

citizen.label: Citizen
citizen.title: Citizens have roles, memory, and snapshot power.
citizen.body: A player can be miner, builder, trader, guardian, author, or auditor. The role does not replace ownership; it explains participation and rule power.

settlement.label: Settlement
settlement.title: Cities and territories are cooperative state.
settlement.body: Settlements can bind chunks, public works, protection coverage, production bonuses, and civic projects into transparent shared accounts.

production.label: Production
production.title: Production is a graph, not magic output.
production.body: Recipes, machines, smelting, forging, and future industry should read explicit input-output rules and preserve old crafted assets.

knowledge.label: Knowledge
knowledge.title: Discoveries become public knowledge records.
knowledge.body: A civilization can unlock technology when exploration, materials, rule text, and validator proof all point to the same discovery.

proof.label: Proof
proof.title: The public browser is part of gameplay.
proof.body: Players should browse citizens, backpacks, settlements, projects, rules, signatures, and decoded PDA data as the trust interface of the world.

epoch.title: Epoch and era registry
epoch.body: Stores active era, effective epoch, unlock rule hash, and compatibility notes for earlier eras.

role.title: Citizen role state
role.body: Stores participation role, maturity, activity score, snapshot weight inputs, and the latest public power hash.

settlement.title: Settlement account
settlement.body: Stores territory links, member policy, public works, production modifiers, and guardian coverage references.

project.title: Civic project account
project.body: Stores contributed resources, goal state, unlock effects, and the rule or settlement that authorized the project.

knowledge.title: Knowledge record account
knowledge.body: Stores discovery claim, material proof, author, text hash, related rule id, and whether it unlocks a future config.

civic.title: Settlements make civilization playable by turning shared work into public state.
civic.body: A civilization system needs more than rule signatures. It needs cooperative projects where players contribute materials, guardians prove coverage, rules authorize effects, and every benefit can be traced back to public accounts.

guarantees.noPrivateTreasury: No hidden treasury: contributed materials should be recorded as receipts, not swallowed by a private server.
guarantees.materialReceipts: Every contribution should preserve resource origin, amount, contributor, project id, and accepted recipe version.
guarantees.guardianProof: Protection or service bonuses require guardian coverage proof for the relevant epoch and territory.
guarantees.ruleAuthorized: A settlement benefit must reference the rule or charter that authorized it.
guarantees.benefitBounded: Benefits are bounded config effects, not arbitrary minting, ownership seizure, or private privilege.

charter.label: Charter
charter.title: A settlement starts with a public charter.
charter.body: The charter explains territory, member policy, public works rights, guardian requirements, and which rules can change the settlement.

contribution.label: Contribution
contribution.title: Materials become receipts before they become power.
contribution.body: Players can see who contributed copper, wood, stone, fuel, or crafted parts, and which milestone accepted the input.

guardian.label: Guardian
guardian.title: Service coverage is proven, not assumed.
guardian.body: Guardian records should show coverage area, epoch, validator result, and whether the project can claim protection-related benefits.

project.label: Project
project.title: Public works progress through milestones.
project.body: Roads, furnaces, archives, markets, defenses, or future machines should have explicit goals, accepted inputs, and completion events.

benefit.label: Benefit
benefit.title: Settlement bonuses are narrow and forward-looking.
benefit.body: A completed project can unlock future production or visibility effects, but it cannot rewrite old assets or create resources without inputs.

audit.label: Audit
audit.title: Anyone can inspect why the settlement changed.
audit.body: The browser should link charter, receipts, guardian proof, rule authorization, milestones, and final effect into one readable chain.

buildSpec.title: This page is also the implementation brief for the future civilization program.
buildSpec.body: Future coding work should implement the smallest trustworthy loop first: publish a hash-bound rule book, sign with frozen civilization power, validate a bounded patch, execute it without a privileged wallet, and expose every PDA in the browser.

program.title: Civilization program surface
program.body: Start with a dedicated program that owns the rule accounts and only writes narrow configuration outputs.

items.stateAccounts: Implement RuleBook, RuleSignature, CitizenState, RuleRegistry, and module config account layouts.
items.instructions: Implement publish_rule, sign_rule, finalize_threshold, execute_rule, and challenge_rule as separate instructions.
items.errors: Return explicit error codes for stale hash, unsafe patch, bad snapshot, wrong scope, and insufficient threshold.

validator.title: Deterministic safety engine
validator.body: Treat the validator as contract logic, not frontend advice.

items.patchParser: Parse a fixed binary patch envelope; reject unknown operations and oversized payloads.
items.safetyGates: Run scope, conservation, asset safety, threshold, cooling, and challenge gates before any write.
items.moduleAdapters: Add one adapter per module so smelting, resource drops, guardian policy, and market policy have explicit field rules.

client.title: Readable governance interface
client.body: The UI must make signing understandable without hiding the machine patch.

items.ruleEditor: Show human text, machine diff, target modules, risk level, old_value_hash, and new_value_hash side by side.
items.signaturePanel: Show snapshot power, signer side, threshold progress, cooling window, and challenge state.
items.pdaBrowser: Expose every rule, signature, citizen snapshot, registry, and config PDA in the existing contract browser.

audit.title: Public verification tools
audit.body: Every successful or failed rule should be easy to reconstruct after the fact.

items.hashCompare: Provide a hash comparison panel for text_hash, patch_hash, old_value_hash, and written config hash.
items.simulation: Provide a dry-run validator output before signatures are collected.
items.eventLog: Log published, signed, finalized, challenged, executed, and rejected events with public reasons.

mockFirst.title: Mock data spec
mockFirst.body: Freeze the JSON/account shapes in UI first so reviewers can challenge the model before chain code exists.

readOnlyPdas.title: Read-only PDA browser
readOnlyPdas.body: Add decoders and account panels before enabling writes, so transparency arrives before power.

signatureFlow.title: Signature flow
signatureFlow.body: Implement rule_signature_pda and threshold recounting with frozen snapshot power.

validatorDryRun.title: Validator dry run
validatorDryRun.body: Run deterministic patch checks without writing config to prove reject reasons.

permissionlessExecute.title: Permissionless execute
permissionlessExecute.body: Enable execute_rule only after hashes, threshold, cooling, challenge, and target config state all pass.

layers.title: A world constitution and an evolving civilization layer.
layers.body: Natural Law protects the world from arbitrary authority. Civilization Law lets players evolve production, construction, craft, and society without turning governance into capital voting.

natural.title: Natural Law
natural.subtitle: Constitution-level consensus for the deepest fairness rules: conservation, asset protection, world generation, NCK principles, and zero project-side privilege.

civilization.title: Civilization Law
civilization.subtitle: Open evolution for production, building, craft, resource processing, non-constitutional parameters, and society-level rules.

classifier.title: The first question is what the rule can touch, not who wants it.
classifier.body: NiceChunk should classify every proposal before signatures count. Future-only configuration can use Civilization Law; old assets, world invariants, supply rules, or privileged paths require Natural Law or immediate rejection.

checks.touchesOldAssets: Does it seize, downgrade, migrate, or rewrite existing player-owned assets?
checks.changesSupply: Does it change resource conservation, generated supply, NCK principles, or world genesis assumptions?
checks.grantsPrivilege: Does it grant one address a bypass, mint path, or private production advantage?
checks.futureOnly: Is the change forward-only and limited to a declared module configuration?
checks.knownAdapter: Can a known module adapter parse and validate every field path in the machine patch?

futureConfig.title: Future-only configuration
futureConfig.body: New recipes, future block parameters, knowledge records, or module policy changes can use normal Civilization Law when old assets are untouched.

oldAssets.title: Existing player assets
oldAssets.body: Any retroactive change to backpacks, listings, ownership, durability, or already-created value must be treated as Natural Law.

coreInvariant.title: World invariants
coreInvariant.body: World generation, conservation, NCK principles, and zero-admin assumptions require the highest consensus path.

privilegeMint.title: Privilege or inflation attempt
privilegeMint.body: A rule that creates value without bounded input or gives one address a private bypass should be rejected before voting.

unknownPatch.title: Unknown machine patch
unknownPatch.body: If the contract cannot parse the adapter, operation, or field path deterministically, the proposal must return to draft.

conservation.title: A credible economy must prove where every new output came from.
conservation.body: Resource rules, recipes, forging, smelting, and civic rewards should all pass the same economic invariant: outputs must be backed by generated origin, consumed inputs, versioned transformation, and public receipts.

checks.generatedSource: Generated resources must point to a world rule, seed, chunk, or drop source that any reader can verify.
checks.inputBurn: Crafting, smelting, forging, and civic rewards must prove required inputs were consumed exactly once.
checks.recipeBound: The output must be bound to the active recipe or rule version, not to a private server decision.
checks.noDuplicateOutput: The same input receipt cannot be replayed to create multiple outputs.
checks.oldAssetProtection: Existing backpacks, listings, and player-owned assets stay protected unless a Natural Law path explicitly authorizes the migration.

source.title: Origin is declared
source.body: A newly generated ore, plant, block, or reward starts with a public origin hash, so players know which world rule created it.

input.title: Inputs are selected
input.body: The transaction names the inventory items, settlement materials, or resource receipts that will fund the transformation.

burn.title: Inputs are consumed
burn.body: The validator records the burn or lock event before output exists, preventing the same input from being reused later.

transform.title: Recipe version is fixed
transform.body: The active config PDA supplies the recipe, smelting table, forging rule, or civic reward formula used for this exact output.

output.title: Output receives a receipt
output.body: The new item or resource receives a receipt hash that binds owner, amount, recipe, source, and execution rule.

audit.title: Balance can be replayed
audit.body: A public conservation ledger lets a browser recompute total inputs, total outputs, rejected attempts, and version changes.

constitution.title: Natural Law is not a normal vote.
constitution.body: A Natural Law change must face stronger prompts, stricter validators, longer cooling, and broader challenge windows because it can affect the deepest fairness assumptions of the world.

checks.stricterValidation: Validator rejects privilege, inflation, and ownership seizure
checks.strongerPrompt: Players must explicitly confirm they understand the rule
checks.widerChallenge: Longer challenge window for auditors and citizens

power.title: Power comes from real participation, not wallet count or raw NCK balance.
power.body: The future contract should weight citizens by world participation while limiting abuse through maturity, caps, active windows, decay, and anomaly penalties.

sources.registration: Registered citizen state and mature world identity
sources.mining: Mined blocks with verifiable generated sources
sources.guardianService: Guardian service coverage and proof submission
sources.ruleReading: Reading and understanding rule books before signing

snapshotIntegrity.title: Civilization power should be computed like an audit trail, not estimated like a leaderboard.
snapshotIntegrity.body: A credible vote needs a frozen, replayable power snapshot. The system should accept only receipt-backed participation, apply eligibility gates, normalize categories, cap concentration, freeze the epoch, and let anyone recount signatures from public accounts.

checks.receiptOnly: Only public receipts count. A website score, private analytics row, or off-chain promise cannot become voting power by itself.
checks.categoryBalance: Mining, forging, settlement work, trading, and audit work should be normalized so one activity cannot dominate every rule.
checks.capClusters: Wallet caps and suspicious-cluster caps reduce cheap account splitting and capital concentration.
checks.epochFreeze: Once a rule is published, the snapshot_epoch freezes signer power so later activity cannot rewrite the vote.
checks.publicRecount: Thresholds must be recomputable from citizen snapshot PDAs and rule_signature_pda accounts.

receipts.title: Collect public receipts
receipts.body: Mining, forging, settlement contribution, market settlement, guardian service, and audit actions produce receipts before they can affect power.

eligibility.title: Apply citizen eligibility
eligibility.body: The account must pass maturity, activity window, identity state, and anomaly filters before any receipt contributes weight.

normalize.title: Normalize categories
normalize.body: Different participation types are converted through a public weight table so raw action counts do not become governance control.

cap.title: Cap concentration
cap.body: Single-wallet caps and suspicious-cluster caps limit both capital capture and cheap wallet splitting.

freeze.title: Freeze the epoch
freeze.body: The snapshot PDA stores the final power hash for an epoch. Rule signatures reference that epoch instead of a mutable live score.

recount.title: Recount from signatures
recount.body: Anyone can sum rule_signature_pda accounts against the frozen snapshot and verify the threshold without trusting the website.

threatModel.title: A civilization system is credible only if it explains how bad rules fail.
threatModel.body: Governance is dangerous when voting can bypass physics, ownership, or transparency. NiceChunk civilization rules should treat every proposal as untrusted until hashes, snapshots, validators, challenge windows, and PDA scopes prove otherwise.

defenses.identity: Civilization power is snapshot-based and filtered by maturity, activity, caps, decay, and anomaly penalties.
defenses.hash: The readable rule, machine patch, old state, and new state are bound by hashes before signatures count.
defenses.validator: RuleValidator runs deterministic checks for scope, conservation, old assets, thresholds, and writable PDA families.
defenses.challenge: Cooling and challenge windows give auditors time to detect misleading text, unsafe patches, or stale state.
defenses.publicFailure: A rejected execution should emit a named reason so users can see why the rule did not mutate state.

sybil.label: Sybil pressure
sybil.title: Many fresh wallets try to manufacture consensus.
sybil.body: New identities should not immediately become full civilization power. Maturity, activity history, caps, and anomaly penalties reduce cheap wallet splitting.

whaleCapture.label: Power capture
whaleCapture.title: One actor tries to dominate votes with raw capital.
whaleCapture.body: The design separates participation-derived civilization power from simple wallet count or raw NCK balance, then caps and decays abnormal concentration.

stalePatch.label: Stale patch
stalePatch.title: A rule signs old state and later overwrites newer config.
stalePatch.body: old_value_hash must match the current target config before execution. If the config has changed, the rule fails instead of overwriting newer work.

assetSeizure.label: Asset seizure
assetSeizure.title: A proposal tries to downgrade or take existing player assets.
assetSeizure.body: Retroactive ownership or value changes are treated as Natural Law territory with stronger prompts, stricter validation, and higher consensus.

resourceInflation.label: Inflation
resourceInflation.title: A patch tries to create resources without bounded input.
resourceInflation.body: Conservation checks require generated source, input burn, or validated transformation before new outputs can enter gameplay.

hiddenAdmin.label: Hidden admin
hiddenAdmin.title: Execution secretly depends on a project-side wallet.
hiddenAdmin.body: execute_rule should be callable by anyone after public conditions pass. A privileged executor would contradict the civilization model.

challenge.title: A player needs a public way to stop a suspicious rule before it executes.
challenge.body: Trust is stronger when disagreement has a procedure. A challenge path should show the review window, evidence hash, execution pause, named rejection reason, and final public outcome.

checks.clearWindow: The rule must expose a clear review and challenge window before execution becomes possible.
checks.evidenceHash: A challenge should cite a stable evidence hash, decoded PDA, or reproducible validator result.
checks.executionPause: Execution must pause while an unresolved challenge is attached to the rule identity.
checks.namedReason: Resolution should publish a named reason instead of a private moderation decision.
checks.publicOutcome: The final accepted or rejected state should remain replayable from public events and accounts.

detect.title: Detect a suspicious rule
detect.body: A player, builder, or auditor sees misleading text, unsafe scope, stale hashes, or missing asset protection.

evidence.title: Attach evidence
evidence.body: The challenge references a hash, decoded account, validator output, or state diff that another reviewer can recompute.

file.title: File the challenge
file.body: The dispute becomes a public object bound to the same rule id, patch hash, challenger, and review window.

pause.title: Pause execution
pause.body: The rule cannot write config PDAs until the challenge is resolved or expires under published conditions.

resolve.title: Resolve with a reason
resolve.body: A valid resolution names the accepted evidence, rejected evidence, or validator gate that decides the outcome.

learn.title: Keep the lesson public
learn.body: Events and decoded accounts make the failure pattern visible so future proposals can avoid the same issue.

caseWalkthrough.title: A Bronze Age rule shows how civilization becomes executable code.
caseWalkthrough.body: This example is written as the coding target for the future civilization program: one player-readable rule, one machine patch, one signature path, one validator result, and one public PDA write.

player.title: The rule unlocks bronze without touching old backpacks.
player.body: Players read it as a civilization advancement: copper and tin can become bronze after the community approves the process.

items.one: Existing copper, tin, tools, and backpacks remain owned by their current players.
items.two: New bronze output requires declared inputs and a validated smelting path.
items.three: A player can inspect the final PDA and see which rule made bronze active.

builder.title: The patch is a bounded module diff.
builder.body: The implementation target is not a free-form text command. It is a structured diff against smelting and forging configuration surfaces.

items.one: Add smelting recipe: copper_ore + tin_ore + heat_tier_2 -> bronze_ingot.
items.two: Add forging recipe: bronze_ingot + handle_material -> bronze_tool_head.
items.three: Reject the patch if field_path, operation, or module adapter is unknown.

auditor.title: Every claim can be replayed from public evidence.
auditor.body: An auditor compares text_hash, patch_hash, old_value_hash, signature PDAs, and execution events before trusting the outcome.

items.two: Check old_value_hash against the current target config before execution.
items.three: Confirm RuleExecuted names the exact written PDA and new_value_hash.

contract.title: execute_rule writes only the declared configuration accounts.
contract.body: The contract validates identity, threshold, cooldown, challenge state, scope, conservation, and asset safety before any write.

items.one: Writable accounts are limited to rule_registry_pda and declared module config PDAs.
items.two: Backpack PDA is read only for asset-safety reasoning, never rewritten by this Civilization Law.
items.three: Failure emits RuleRejected with a stable failed_gate instead of partial state.

textHash.title: Readable rule identity
textHash.body: The human explanation that players sign must hash to this value.

patchHash.title: Machine patch identity
patchHash.body: The executable patch bytes must hash to this value before signatures count.

oldHash.title: Expected current config
oldHash.body: Execution stops if the live smelting or forging config no longer matches the reviewed state.

newHash.title: Committed target config
newHash.body: The accepted patch produces this new config hash after validator gates pass.

writtenPda.title: Narrow writable surface
writtenPda.body: The transaction may write only the declared config PDAs, not arbitrary player or market accounts.

event.title: Public execution record
event.body: Indexers and the website can show the same event as proof that the rule became active.

impact.title: Before signing, a player should see the rule's personal, world, and technical impact.
impact.body: A trustworthy civilization interface should simulate a proposal before it becomes power: what changes for my backpack, what changes for future production, which PDAs are read, which hashes must match, and why the validator accepts or rejects the patch.

checks.readAccounts: Read the declared accounts without mutating state: rule book, target configs, backpacks, settlement effects, and citizen snapshots.
checks.compareHashes: Compare text_hash, patch_hash, old_value_hash, and expected new_value_hash before anyone signs.
checks.showDiff: Show the human-readable before/after diff beside the machine patch field path.
checks.simulateFailure: Show the exact rejection reason if a gate fails: stale hash, unsafe asset migration, conservation break, wrong scope, or missing threshold.
checks.explainChoice: Translate the dry-run result into a clear agree, reject, or abstain decision for nontechnical players.

personal.label: Personal
personal.title: Will this touch my wallet or backpack?
personal.body: The simulator should say whether the rule reads my accounts, writes my accounts, or has no direct personal effect.

assets.label: Assets
assets.title: Can old assets be changed?
assets.body: Existing backpacks, listings, crafted items, and ownership records must be shown as protected, read-only, migrated, or blocked by Natural Law.

production.label: Production
production.title: What future gameplay changes?
production.body: Players should see which recipes, drop tables, machine rules, or block parameters change after the rule becomes active.

settlement.label: Settlement
settlement.title: Does the rule affect a civic project?
settlement.body: Settlement changes should show affected territory, project milestones, guardian coverage, and forward-only benefits.

economy.label: Economy
economy.title: Does value appear from nowhere?
economy.body: The dry run should explain input requirements, resource conservation, fee effects, and why no private address receives hidden privilege.

technical.label: Technical
technical.title: Which gate accepts or rejects the patch?
technical.body: Professional users should see parsed operations, account owners, writable PDAs, adapter names, and deterministic validator output.

signing.title: A signature should feel like a public commitment, not a blind button press.
signing.body: The signing screen should prove that the player is signing the same rule text, same machine patch, same frozen power snapshot, same risk summary, and one clear side: agree, reject, or abstain.
signing.stackTitle: What the wallet must make explicit

checks.sameObject: The screen must show the exact text_hash and patch_hash that the wallet signs.
checks.snapshotPower: The signer sees the frozen snapshot_epoch and power_at_snapshot before choosing a side.
checks.sideMeaning: Agree, reject, and abstain must be described as different public commitments, not cosmetic buttons.
checks.riskPrompt: High-risk rules need stronger prompts that name affected assets, economy, authority, and timing.
checks.revocationLimit: If a vote can be replaced before the window closes, the replacement rule must be explicit and auditable.

asset.title: Asset impact
asset.body: Does the rule only read player assets, affect future assets, migrate old assets, or require Natural Law protection?

economy.title: Economic impact
economy.body: Does the dry-run prove conservation, input consumption, fee movement, and no hidden recipient?

authority.title: Authority impact
authority.body: Which PDA families can be written, and does any address receive privilege outside the published patch?

timing.title: Timing impact
timing.body: When can the rule execute, how long is the challenge window, and what state can change before execution?

personal.title: Personal scope
personal.body: Does this signature read my wallet only, use my power, or authorize any write touching my accounts?

irreversible.title: Finality impact
irreversible.body: What happens after execution, how can a future rule supersede it, and what cannot be undone?

side.title: Signer side
side.body: The signature PDA records the exact side so agree, reject, and abstain can be counted separately.

power.title: Frozen power
power.body: The signer contributes the power stored at the referenced snapshot, not a mutable live balance.

snapshot.title: Snapshot epoch
snapshot.body: Every signature points to the same epoch so threshold math can be replayed.

text.title: Text identity
text.body: The human-readable rule text is bound to the signature by hash.

patch.title: Patch identity
patch.body: The executable patch hash is bound to the same signature so the signed text cannot hide different bytes.

risk.title: Risk acknowledgement
risk.body: The signer acknowledges the displayed risk summary, making later disputes about hidden warnings easier to audit.

decision.title: Reject, abstain, and challenge are different tools, not the same kind of no.
decision.body: A trustworthy governance screen must teach players the difference between support, opposition, silence, and evidence-based blocking. Each action should write a different public meaning while sharing the same rule identity.
decision.stackTitle: How to read the four choices

checks.oneIdentity: All four actions reference the same rule_id, text_hash, patch_hash, snapshot_epoch, and risk summary.
checks.differentMeanings: Agree and reject are threshold math; abstain records participation without adding yes or no pressure.
checks.challengeEvidence: A challenge is not just a stronger no. It must attach evidence and can pause execution.
checks.publicMath: Anyone should be able to recount yes_power, no_power, abstain_power, and unresolved challenges from public accounts.
checks.clearOutcome: The interface should show whether the rule is finalized, paused by challenge, rejected, or still waiting.

agree.label: Support
agree.title: Agree means I want this rule to pass.
agree.body: The signer adds frozen power to yes_power. It does not skip cooling, challenge windows, or validator gates.

reject.label: Oppose
reject.title: Reject means I count against the rule.
reject.body: The signer adds frozen power to no_power. It expresses opposition but does not automatically pause execution.

abstain.label: Present
abstain.title: Abstain means I was counted but did not choose yes or no.
abstain.body: The signer records participation and power separately, useful when the player lacks enough confidence to support or oppose.

challenge.label: Dispute
challenge.title: Challenge means I have evidence that execution should stop.
challenge.body: The challenger files a public evidence hash. An unresolved challenge blocks writes until the rule is resolved or expires.

side.title: Side value
side.body: The signature side is a typed value, so UI labels cannot silently reinterpret what a player signed.

signature.title: Signature account
signature.body: One rule signature PDA stores voter, rule, side, snapshot power, and signed hashes.

challenge.title: Challenge account
challenge.body: A challenge has its own PDA because it carries evidence and may pause execution.

pause.title: Pause counter
pause.body: Execution checks unresolved challenges before writing config PDAs.

recount.title: Recount fields
recount.body: Yes, no, and abstain power are public sums over the same snapshot.

outcome.title: Outcome event
outcome.body: Finalization, pause, rejection, and execution should produce named events for indexers and players.

reputation.title: Civilization power should come from provable play, not a transferable vote token.
reputation.body: NiceChunk's civilization layer can turn discovery, backpack ownership, forging, settlement work, and audit challenges into a public citizen snapshot. A player sees why their voice counts; an auditor sees the receipts, caps, decay, and replay path behind that power.
reputation.stackTitle: Why civic power is hard to fake

guards.receiptOnly: Only actions with decoded receipts can enter the score; website-only claims do not count.
guards.snapshotEpoch: Rule signatures use an epoch snapshot, so last-minute account changes cannot rewrite signer power.
guards.capAndDecay: Each source has a cap and decay curve, preventing one activity or one old achievement from dominating forever.
guards.nonTransferable: Power follows the citizen history behind a wallet; it is not a free-transfer vote token that can be bought in one trade.
guards.replayableMath: Indexers, players, and auditors should be able to recompute the same score from public PDA data.

discovery.label: Discovery
discovery.title: NCM exploration creates origin evidence.
discovery.body: Finding and proving valuable chunks can become a civilization signal because the source, seed, and discovery receipt are inspectable.

backpack.label: Backpack
backpack.title: Owned assets prove material participation.
backpack.body: Backpack state shows which resources, crafted items, and historical holdings a citizen actually carried through the world.

forging.label: Forging
forging.title: Craft outputs prove productive work.
forging.body: Smelting and forging receipts show that the player transformed inputs under public recipe rules instead of only holding balance.

market.label: Market
market.title: Settled trades prove honest exchange.
market.body: Market history can reward completed, non-abusive exchange while excluding wash loops and failed settlement records.

settlement.label: Settlement
settlement.title: Civic work proves public contribution.
settlement.body: Material receipts, milestone accounts, and guardian coverage make settlement labor visible without trusting a private treasury.

audit.label: Audit
audit.title: Challenges prove rule-review labor.
audit.body: Valid challenges, resolved evidence, and caught failures can become civic credit because they protect the whole civilization.

lifecycle.title: A rule book moves from writing to execution without project-side permission.

draft.title: Draft Rule Book
draft.body: A player writes readable intent and a machine patch for a target module.

publish.title: Publish On-chain Hash
publish.body: The book fixes text_hash, patch_hash, author, law type, risk level, and snapshot epoch.

read.title: Read & Understand
read.body: Citizens inspect the text, impact scope, old asset policy, and validator warnings.

sign.title: Sign Civilization Power
sign.body: Citizens sign agree, reject, or abstain with snapshot-based civilization power.

threshold.title: Reach Threshold
threshold.body: Civilization Law needs over 50%; Natural Law needs over 90% active civilization power.

cooling.title: Cooling & Challenge
cooling.body: The rule waits through cooling and challenge windows so mistakes can be disputed.

execute.title: Execute Rule
execute.body: Any player can call execute_rule when on-chain conditions are satisfied.

active.title: Rule Becomes Active
active.body: The program writes the approved config into rule registries and module config PDAs.

schema.title: Human-readable intent and machine-executable patch stay bound by hashes.
schema.body: The UI stores mock data locally today. Later these fields can map directly to rule_book_pda, rule_signature_pda, and rule_registry_pda account layouts.

fields.title: Readable rule name shown to players and reviewers.
fields.author: Wallet that published the rule book hash.
fields.category: Natural Law or Civilization Law threshold class.
fields.target_module: One or more configuration modules affected by the patch.
fields.human_readable_text: Readable explanation that players review before signing.
fields.machine_patch: Structured patch bytes consumed by the RuleValidator.
fields.snapshot_epoch: Epoch used to freeze signer power.
fields.status: Lifecycle state from draft to active.

technical.title: The contract executes a verified patch, not a vague social instruction.
technical.body: The human rule explains the intent. The machine patch states exactly which config PDA fields change. The validator rejects unsafe patches before a registry write can happen.

hashBinding.title: Hash binding
hashBinding.body: The readable text and machine patch are committed by hash so a signer can audit whether the thing they read is the thing being executed.

validatorBeforeWrite.title: Validator before write
validatorBeforeWrite.body: Unsafe updates are rejected before they reach module registries, including no-input output, privileged address logic, and ownership seizure.

pdaRegistry.title: PDA registry surface
pdaRegistry.body: Accepted rules write into versioned registry and configuration PDAs that public tools can fetch and decode.

permissionlessExecution.title: Permissionless execution
